evidence_quotes.quote: dark patterns and addictive design being addressed
These unfair practices are common not just in video games but in software industry as a whole, where planned obsolescence is not communicated to the customer in any fair way.
the term 'purchase' is redefined to mean leasing, licensing or subscribing.
reserving all rights to revoke access at any time for no specified reason without any guidelines detailing what may cause such action to be taken.

paragraphs.text: I would like to express my support for the Digital Fairness Act, I'm happy to see dark patterns and addictive design being addressed as I see these as major contributors to unfair treatment of digital customers - I say this as someone who has fallen victim to 'purchase' buttons being stylized the same as 'cancel' buttons, and as someone who is very concerned about prevalence of gambling in online spaces wrapped in layers of currency abstraction. I would also like to see this Act address the practice of publishers and software distributors who mislead their customers by marketing their goods as 'purchasable;' In reality hidden deep in terms of service or purchase agreements the term 'purchase' is redefined to mean leasing, licensing or subscribing. Another practice is these publishers/distributors reserving all rights to revoke access at any time for no specified reason without any guidelines detailing what may cause such action to be taken. This results in the customers losing access to software that they have bought with their money and rightfully assumed to own forever as any other offline good would be. I believe it's an issue that is in the same category as the issues already addressed by this Act and would bring necessary customer protections for fair digital purchases. I would also like to point out that the European Citizen Initiative called "Stop Destroying Videogames" is asking for this misleading practice to be regulated and it has already gained enough votes to be considered by the EU. It is a known concern among users of digital media; These unfair practices are common not just in video games but in software industry as a whole, where planned obsolescence is not communicated to the customer in any fair way.
